back: A set is finite if its cardinality (number of elements) is a nonnegative integer. In other words, there exists a bijection between the set and the set {1, 2, ..., n} for some natural number n.
A set is infinite if it is not finite. This means there is no natural number n such that the set can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with {1, 2, ..., n}.

front: What is the key property that distinguishes infinite sets from finite ones, according to Dedekind?
back: A set is infinite (Dedekind-infinite) if and only if it can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with a proper subset of itself. This is not possible for finite sets.

front: Give an example of a proper subset of an infinite set that has the same cardinality.
back: The set of natural numbers ℕ is infinite. The set of even numbers E = {2, 4, 6, ...} is a proper subset of ℕ. The function f: ℕ → E defined by f(n) = 2n is a bijection, showing that |ℕ| = |E|.

front: How do finite and infinite sets behave differently under set operations like union?
back: The union of two finite sets is always finite. However, the union of an infinite set with any other set (finite or infinite) is infinite.
